findings.title: Friend-invite strips the new companion's linkedUserId server-side (upsert fires before the member row exists) → 'Pending' badge silently reverts to 'Unlinked' and a duplicate chip appears when they accept
findings.rootCause: In the friend-add handler the writes run in the order: emit → `void upsertTrip(trip)` (fires POST /api/trips immediately, carrying the companion with the freshly-set `linkedUserId`) → `await inviteTripMember(...)` (creates the `pending` trip_members row). The server's `_cleaned_companions` (trips.py:42-62) builds `verified_linked_ids` = the set of user_ids currently in `trip_members`, and `clean_companions` (validators.py:447-449) coerces any `linkedUserId` NOT in that set to None. Because the upsert reaches SQLite before the invite has created the member row, `friendId` is not yet a member, so the link is stripped and the companion is persisted as name-only. The local STATE keeps the link optimistically, but `pullFromServer` overwrites `trip.companions` wholesale from the server value (`companions: normalizeTripCompanions(t.companions)` at api.ts:149; only the 4 heavy media fields are merged, never companions), so the next 15s poll clobbers the optimistic link with the stripped server copy. Nothing re-links companions from trip_members on read, so once the friend accepts, `buildRosterChips` can't dedupe the companion against the member (the `linkedUserId` is gone) and renders both.

findings.title: Invite failures (409 role-conflict, 404 blocked/unknown user, 403) are swallowed: companion is still linked + persisted and a false 'invited' success toast shows
findings.rootCause: `inviteTripMember` uses `_post` (api/core.ts:296), which returns the raw Response (or undefined on network error) and never throws. The handler does `await inviteTripMember(trip.id, friendId, role)` (companions.ts:369) but ignores the result entirely, then unconditionally sets the link (lines 353-365), calls upsertTrip, and fires the success toast (line 372). The server returns 409 'Member already accepted with a different role' (trips.py:899-903) and 404 for blocked/nonexistent targets (trips.py:869-876), none of which surface to the user.

findings.title: Budgeteers are mislabelled as 'Relaxer' on the trip header role badge
findings.rootCause: The header renders the badge with `{!tripIsEditable ? <Relaxer badge> : null}` where `tripIsEditable = canEdit(activeTrip)` (TripBody.tsx:138), and `canEdit` is planner-only (permissions.ts:49-51). So for BOTH relaxers and budgeteers `tripIsEditable` is false, and the same hardcoded '👁 Relaxer' badge (companions.relaxerBadgeTitle / roleRelaxer) is shown. There is no branch for the budgeteer role.

findings.title: Linking a friend to a same-named existing unlinked companion silently merges two different people
findings.rootCause: In the brand-new-add branch, `findTripCompanion(trip, friendName)` (companions.ts:360) matches purely by name, case-insensitively (companions.ts:45-57), and if an unlinked row exists it sets `existingByName.linkedUserId = friendId` (line 362). There is no check that the existing unlinked entry actually refers to the same person. Because `addTripCompanion` also dedupes by name (companions.ts:144-150), the roster can never hold two 'Alex' rows, forcing the merge.

findings.title: Companion remove-lock is case-sensitive while the rest of the companion system is case-insensitive — a referenced companion can lose its 🔒 and be removed
findings.rootCause: `referencedNames` is built from raw `e.who` + split keys (companions.ts:69-74) and the lock test is `referencedNames.has(c.name)` (line 93) — an EXACT, case-sensitive Set match. Everywhere else the app treats companion names case-insensitively (findTripCompanion at companions.ts:45-57, and clean_companions case-folds server-side). So 'alice' in an expense vs an 'Alice' companion (e.g. a name typed with different casing into the ManualTab free-text 'who' field, or a CSV upload) won't match the lock, dropping the protection. (Balance math still uses a ghost-roster union so money isn't lost, but the lock — the user-facing guard — is bypassed.)

findings.title: Non-owner Planner can invite and kick other members via the API even though the UI presents roster management as owner-only
findings.rootCause: Server invite gate is `can_edit_trip` (= planner, trips.py:856) and remove gate is `can_edit_trip` for non-self removals (trips.py:1099), only blocking removal of the owner (trips.py:1101). The frontend permission helpers gate the same actions on `isOwner` (permissions.ts:64-79). The two layers disagree on who controls the roster.
